text.title: Dependency-Adoption Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn dependency adoption from a one-line npm install into a defensible audit-ready commit.

rule.statement: The new dependency MUST be pinned to an exact version in both the manifest and the lockfile. Caret (^) and tilde (~) ranges are forbidden for direct deps. The lockfile commit includes the SHA/integrity hash if the ecosystem supports it.

rationale.source: Reproducible-build research; npm/yarn supply-chain incidents (event-stream, ua-parser-js)

rule.rationale: Ranged deps allow silent upgrades that can introduce malware or breaking changes between two CI runs. Exact pin is the floor for reproducibility and audit.

rule.statement: The dependency MUST have a named owner on the team — the human who answers "what is this for?", "is it healthy?", "what's the next upgrade path?". Owner = "the team" is rejected. The owner field is in dependency-adoption.md and is the escalation target for security alerts.

rationale.source: Site Reliability Engineering on toil ownership

rule.rationale: Unowned dependencies rot. Security alerts for unowned deps land in #general and are ignored. Named ownership is the only mechanism that scales.

rule.statement: The new dependency MUST appear in the SBOM produced by the CI pipeline. CI verifies the SBOM contains the new dep with version + license + supplier. SBOM coverage is checked as part of PR review (a separate CI step), not assumed.

rationale.source: NIST SSDF + Executive Order 14028 on SBOM requirements

rule.rationale: SBOM is the audit artifact for supply-chain reviews and is increasingly contractually required. A dep not in SBOM is a future audit finding.

rule.statement: The adoption file MUST contain an escape-hatch section: list of 1-2 replacement candidates with similar API/feature surface, plus a 3-bullet outline of what migration would look like. The escape hatch is written at adoption time, not at panic time when the dep is abandoned/compromised.

rationale.source: Risk-management practice; cf. lessons from left-pad, faker, ua-parser-js

rule.rationale: When a dep gets abandoned or compromised, teams scramble to find a replacement under pressure. A pre-written escape hatch makes the swap a planned exercise rather than an incident.

rule.statement: The adoption file MUST set a renewal-review date (default: 12 months, sooner for high-risk deps). On that date the owner re-evaluates: still maintained? still needed? still pinned? Renewal output is committed as a brief update.

rationale.source: Lifecycle management; deps that adoption-reviewed once never get re-reviewed

rule.rationale: Without scheduled renewal, dead dependencies accumulate. The renewal date is the forcing function for "is this still earning its place?"

rule.statement: If preconditions in AGENTS.md 'Applies If' do not all hold, skip this methodology and route to the correct one; do not retro-fit inputs.
rule.rationale: Forcing a methodology onto a misaligned task produces artefacts downstream consumers cannot trust.

example.title: Adoption-file entry for a new direct dep
example.code: Dependency: @tanstack/react-query Version pin: 5.59.0 (lockfile sha512: c4f...) Manifest: package.json Owner: Anna L. (frontend team lead) Adopted: 2026-05-20 SBOM entry: confirmed in dist/sbom.json @ 2026-05-20T15:12Z Escape hatch: - Candidates: SWR (similar fetcher caching), Apollo Client (if moving to GraphQL) - Migration outline: extract useQuery to a thin wrapper; abstract refetch policies; estimate 3 days for current usage size. Renewal review: 2027-05-01 Risk notes: actively maintained (last release 14 days), BUS-factor good (multiple core contributors), MIT license.

example.title: Quick adoption
example.code: "npm install --save react-query" PR title: "Add react-query" PR body: "Better data fetching"
example.note: Ranged pin (caret-default), no owner, no SBOM check, no escape hatch, no renewal. Classic accidental adoption.
